lessons.title: Light
lessons.shortDescription: How to find good light in 30 seconds

steps.title: Hard vs Soft light
steps.description:
Hard light creates harsh shadows under the eyes and nose; the face looks tired.

Soft light keeps shadows gentle and skin looks better. For people, almost always choose soft light.

steps.title: Where light should come from
steps.description: The best light is in front of the face and slightly from the side. Light from above or behind makes the face dark and flat.

steps.title: Where to look for good light
steps.text:
Find a spot where the face is brighter than the background. Usually:
• near a window or storefront
• in building shade, facing open sky

Avoid places where light is above or behind you—under lamps or in direct sun on the back of the head.
steps.exampleImageLabel: Face the light—that’s how you pick the spot

lessons.title: Horizon
lessons.shortDescription: How to keep the horizon straight

steps.title: Why the horizon should be level
steps.description:
A tilted horizon is one of the most common mistakes.
We rarely notice a small tilt while shooting, but in the photo it stands out immediately.

steps.title: Level the horizon with the grid
steps.text:
Turn on the camera grid.

Before you shoot, align the horizon with a grid line.
If you can’t see the horizon, use buildings, poles, or walls—they should look vertical.

lessons.title: Framing
lessons.shortDescription: How to frame a photo cleanly

steps.title: What a good frame means
steps.description: A good frame has everything important in it and nothing distracting. The subject should be the clear focus.

steps.title: Framing and a clean frame
steps.text: Move closer or use zoom. Remove clutter—trash, random people, objects that don’t belong in the story.

lessons.title: Café portrait
lessons.shortDescription: How to photograph someone in a café

steps.title: Why cafés work well for portraits
steps.description: Cafés usually have nice light and a pleasant background. It’s a great setting for a portrait.

steps.title: Shooting a café portrait
steps.text: Seat the person by the window so light falls on the face. Use the café as background—coffee, desserts. Hold the phone at eye level.

steps.title: How to make a good group photo
steps.description: Everyone should be visible and in focus. Nobody should be awkwardly cropped out.

steps.title: How to arrange the group
steps.text: Step back so everyone fits. Ask people to stand in one row or two (taller people in the back). Make sure every face is visible.
